nativeRange.biotope: The Flowerhorn is captive-bred only and has no wild population. It was developed in Malaysia and Taiwan during the 1990s and 2000s through multi-generational crosses of Central American cichlid species, principally Amphilophus citrinellus, A. trimaculatus, and Vieja synspilum, among others. Because it has never existed in the wild, no natural biotope can be assigned to it.

decorPreferences: large smooth river rocks as territory markers and cave backdrop
minimal or no live plants (will uproot and destroy planting)
sandy or fine-gravel substrate — easy to clean and gentle on the kok

diet.notes: Accepts high-quality large cichlid pellets as the staple; supplement with frozen or live foods: large earthworms, crickets, krill, frozen bloodworms, shrimp. Colour-enhancing pellets with astaxanthin or spirulina support the vivid red-orange body colouration and the kok. Feed 1–2 times daily and remove uneaten food promptly — Flowerhorns are voracious and produce significant waste. Avoid over-feeding, which rapidly degrades water quality in the single-specimen tank. Do not feed mammalian meat (beef heart) as a staple; the saturated fats contribute to fatty-liver disease.

goodWith: large robust catfish as tank janitors in very large tanks (e.g., common pleco, Sailfin pleco) — with caution and sufficient space
large armoured catfish (Pseudacanthicus, Pterygoplichthys) in 600 L+ setups
avoidWith: virtually all other fish — Flowerhorns are best kept alone
conspecifics (extreme intraspecific aggression; same-species kills are common)
invertebrates (shrimp, snails — will be eaten or harassed)
any fish small enough to be consumed
any fish that cannot hold its own against sustained attack

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by Lerdsuwa; 2000x1229. Textbook lateral profile of a whole adult Flowerhorn: vivid red-orange body, prominent cranial kok, pearl-spot flanks and the black horizontal midline blotches. Sharp, well-lit, clean blue background. Best diagnostic image.

Photo by Arclink; 7008x4672. Striking full-body shot of a large-kok Flowerhorn under blue display lighting; crisp pearl patterning and big nuchal hump. Non-recommended for its stylised lighting shifting the body toward magenta.
Photo by Garry Knight; 5120x3840. Sharp, colourful three-quarter view showing a vivid red kok and heavy pearl-spotting. Non-recommended because the head-forward angle crops the body foreshortened.

summary: The Flowerhorn is an entirely artificial freshwater cichlid hybrid, developed in Malaysia and Taiwan in the 1990s and 2000s from crosses of several Central American cichlid species. It is prized for its dramatic cranial hump, known as the kok, its vivid colouration, and its unusually interactive personality — though its intense aggression means it is almost always kept alone.
careNotes:
The Flowerhorn Cichlid is an entirely captive-created hybrid with no wild equivalent, descended from multi-generational crosses of Amphilophus citrinellus, A. trimaculatus, Vieja synspilum, and other Central American cichlids.

A minimum tank of 380 L (approximately 100 US gallons) and 150 cm in length is widely cited for a single adult. The Flowerhorn is extraordinarily territorial and also unusually interactive — it tends to track its keeper's movements, can be hand-fed, and is commonly kept as a single showpiece specimen. Attempting to house multiple Flowerhorns together or keeping them with most other fish almost always results in casualties. In very large tanks of 600 L or more, a robust pleco or large armoured catfish may coexist, but this requires careful monitoring.

The prized cranial kok (the nuchal hump on top of the head) is androgen-dependent and tends to be larger and more pronounced in males. Kok size varies considerably between lines: Kamfa and Golden Monkey lines are known for especially large koks, while ZZ (Zhen Zhu) and Red Texas lines are prized for colouration and pearl-spot patterning. Because Flowerhorns are hybrids, most individuals are sterile, though some pairings can produce viable fry when a fertile male Flowerhorn is paired with a compatible female cichlid under carefully controlled conditions.

Water quality is critical: the Flowerhorn is a high-waste producer. Oversized mechanical and biological filtration — commonly a filter rated for three to four times the tank volume — and weekly partial water changes of 25–30% are the norm. Temperature toward the upper end of the range (28–30°C) is preferred by most keepers and is consistent with the Central American cichlid ancestry. A pH of 7.0–8.0 with moderate hardness (gH 8–20) suits both the ancestral species' requirements and the broad tolerance of the hybrid lines.
breedingNotes: Most Flowerhorn cichlids are sterile hybrids and do not readily reproduce. Occasional fertile individuals exist, typically in first- or second-generation crosses that retain greater genetic proximity to the parent species. When fertile, Flowerhorns are substrate spawners: the pair excavates a pit or lays eggs on a flat surface (slate, rock), and both parents guard the clutch aggressively. Pair aggression is severe — introductions must be done through a divider over several weeks, and the female must be removed immediately if the male becomes dangerously aggressive. Successful spawning has been documented between fertile male Flowerhorns and female Amphilophus citrinellus or other compatible cichlid species. Fry are free-swimming within 4–5 days and accept finely crushed cichlid pellet and baby brine shrimp nauplii from the start.

primary.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Flowerhorn Cichlid Care Guide
primary.notes: Primary trade source. Provided tank size (100 gal minimum for single specimen), temperature (78–86°F / 26–30°C), pH (7.0–8.0), diet (pellets, earthworms, crickets, krill), aggression profile (single-specimen recommended), careLevel intermediate, and hybrid origin summary. Cross-checked against SeriouslyFish, FishBase, and Practical Fishkeeping.

additional.name: SeriouslyFish — Flowerhorn Cichlid
additional.notes: Confirmed hybrid origin (Amphilophus citrinellus × trimaculatus with Vieja synspilum ancestry), adult size to ~30–35 cm, temperature 26–30°C, pH 7.0–8.0, hardness tolerance, single-specimen keeping, and aggression notes. Also confirmed fw-fish-349 (null scientificName) is a duplicate entry — same fish, merge candidate.

additional.name: FishBase — Amphilophus citrinellus (parent species reference)
additional.notes: Used for parent-species taxonomy (Cichlidae, Cichliformes), ancestral water parameter cross-check (temperature 22–28°C, pH 6.5–8.0, hardness moderate), and geographic origin (Central America: Nicaragua, Costa Rica). FishBase has no entry for the Flowerhorn itself as it is an artificial hybrid with no valid binomial — scientificName is therefore recorded as the informal hybrid notation 'Amphilophus citrinellus × trimaculatus (hybrid)'.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Flowerhorn: the fish that feng shui built
additional.notes: Provided cultural and historical background: development in Malaysia/Taiwan late 1990s, feng shui symbolism, named lines (Kamfa, ZZ/Zhen Zhu, Red Texas, Golden Monkey), kok genetics and androgen dependency, and hobbyist context for single-specimen display keeping.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — Flowerhorn Cichlids: An Overview
additional.notes: Supplementary source on breeding line distinctions, sterility of most hybrids, occasional fertile individuals in early-generation crosses, and pair-introduction technique via tank dividers. Confirmed lifespan of 8–12 years under good conditions and high-waste output requiring oversized filtration.

fish.breedingNotes: Most individuals are sterile hybrids. Occasional fertile specimens exist, typically in first- or second-generation crosses that retain closer genetic proximity to the parent species. When fertile, Flowerhorns are biparental substrate-spawners: the pair excavates a pit or deposits eggs on a flat surface, and both parents guard the clutch. Pair introduction must be done slowly through a tank divider over several weeks due to extreme aggression, and the female may need to be removed after spawning if the male becomes dangerously aggressive. Fry accept finely crushed pellet and baby brine shrimp nauplii from the free-swimming stage.
